_note: Glass Box demo GALLERY (RAILS MODE replay). Seven authored questions, no category buckets. Three carry `hidden: true` (counterparty_exposure, salesforce_blast_radius, denied_claims_driver): /api/demo/questions omits them so the UI picker does not show them, but they are RETAINED here and /api/demo/trace?q=<id> still resolves them — removed from the UI, NOT deleted. The first two are AGENTIC and tag every node with the system it runs in (`system`: contextOS / agent / hitl) so the agent↔contextOS boundary is explicit. `agent_rightsize` (contextOS relates cost x output across sources so the agent finds the worst $/deploy team — data_sci_apac — then pinpoints its idle instances; the cross-source relate is contextOS, the query + pinpoint are the agent). `agent_execute` (the core function — the FinOps agent right-sizes a CONCRETE underutilized EC2 instance, i-75c0c1813079025d c5.2xlarge -> c5.large, gated by HITL Head-of-Cloud-Platform approval; NOT autonomous, NOT a 'team' right-size). The other five are read-queries. The cross-source numbers (data_sci_apac, $1,145.18/deploy, aws_cost_explorer x deploy_analytics, entity CloudFleet-USE1-7f3a) mirror the real headless agent-arc capture; the EC2 rightsize (instance, c5.2xlarge->c5.large, 22%->85%, ~$1K/mo, approval) mirrors the FinOps Recommendations 'Approve Optimization' surface. Each question carries: (a) `story.beats` — the authored narrative + raw records (the live-engine beat contract, served unchanged by backend/api/routes/demo.py); and (b) `graph` — the node-graph topology the React-Flow Glass Box animates (intake -> source-system candidates -> link/verify -> reducer; agentic questions end in a `graph.action` ACT node instead of the reducer, and every node may carry a `system` lane tag). The source system (workday, murex, …) is shown as the candidate NODE itself; a verdict of 'excised' severs that path. Authored replay, NOT live engine output; no silent fallback. The `graph` source-system labels and the eventual record lineage must be wired to real sources in prod.

questions.question: Which team is burning the most per deploy?

beats.text: A FinOps agent connects to AOS read-only — scoped to cloud_spend as finops-rightsizing — and asks one question across the whole estate.
Billing shows $128,914 of cloud spend, but it can't say which team is wasteful — billing has no notion of output.
contextOS relates two systems billing never sees together — cost from aws_cost_explorer, deploys from deploy_analytics — so the agent can rank every team by dollars per deploy.
data_sci_apac burns $1,145.18 per deploy — the worst in the estate. contextOS hands the agent that signal; the agent pinpoints the underutilized instances behind it to right-size next.

answer.headline: data_sci_apac · $1,145.18 / deploy
answer.sub: $21,758.42 in cloud cost (AWS Cost Explorer) across 19 deploys (Deploy Analytics) — $1,145.18 per deploy, the highest of any team.

candidates.label: Source: Billing
candidates.excisedDetail: One system, no output dimension — can't rank efficiency. Excised.

action.label: Pinpoints the idle instances
action.detail: data_sci_apac's underutilized instances flagged → ready to right-size

answer.headline: data_sci_apac · $1,145.18 / deploy
answer.sub: $21,758.42 in cloud cost (AWS Cost Explorer) across 19 deploys (Deploy Analytics) — $1,145.18 per deploy, the highest of any team.

questions.question: Right-size the idle EC2 instance the agent flagged.

beats.text: The agent takes contextOS's signal — data_sci_apac is the worst $/deploy team — and drills into the instances behind it.
One EC2 instance, i-75c0c1813079025d, is a c5.2xlarge running at 22% utilization — $7,561 a month for capacity it isn't using.
The agent proposes the fix: downsize to c5.large — expected ~85% utilization, about $1K a month saved ($15K a year), under 5% performance risk.
Right-sizing production isn't autonomous here — it needs Head of Cloud Platform approval. Approved, the agent executes the downsize in the Sun 2-4 AM maintenance window.

answer.headline: Right-sized i-75c0c… → c5.large
answer.sub: i-75c0c1813079025d was a c5.2xlarge at 22% utilization ($7,561/mo). Downsized to c5.large — ~85% expected utilization, ~$1,000/mo saved. Approved by Head of Cloud Platform.

action.label: Head of Cloud Platform approves → executed
action.detail: HITL gate · downsized in the Sun 2-4 AM window · cited to CloudWatch + AWS Cost Explorer

answer.headline: Right-sized i-75c0c… → c5.large
answer.sub: i-75c0c1813079025d was a c5.2xlarge at 22% utilization ($7,561/mo). Downsized to c5.large — ~85% expected utilization, ~$1,000/mo saved. Approved by Head of Cloud Platform.

questions.question: What's the real run-rate for Engineering?

beats.text: Two systems disagree on Engineering's run-rate.
Workday — your system of record — says $2.64M.
A shadow CRM claims $8.64M — but that record is cancelled, from an unauthorized source.
So the $8.64M is dropped before the LLM ever sees it.

answer.headline: $2.64M
answer.sub: From Workday (system of record). The conflicting $8.64M from shadow_crm is a cancelled record — excluded.

answer.headline: $2.64M
answer.sub: From Workday (system of record). The conflicting $8.64M from shadow_crm is a cancelled record — excluded.

questions.question: What's our real exposure to Brightwater Partners?

beats.text: Two trading desks each face Brightwater Partners.
Prime brokerage holds $320M. Financing holds another $420M.
They're booked under two different legal entities — that roll up to the same parent. Nobody had added them together.

record.children: Brightwater Markets Ltd, Brightwater Capital LLC

beats.text: After netting, your true exposure is far bigger than either desk saw.

answer.headline: $612M net
answer.sub: Murex prime brokerage ($320M) and Calypso financing ($420M) book under two legal entities that roll up to Brightwater — $612M net of collateral.

link.label: Legal-Entity Rollup
link.detail: Two entities → one parent. Netted across desks.

answer.headline: $612M net
answer.sub: Murex prime brokerage ($320M) and Calypso financing ($420M) book under two legal entities that roll up to Brightwater — $612M net of collateral.

questions.question: If Salesforce goes down, what actually breaks?

beats.text: Salesforce feeds 14 live integrations.
One of them drives your quote-to-cash service.
And the month-end financial close quietly depends on a nightly Salesforce export — with no runbook and no on-call owner.

answer.headline: 9 systems · 2 teams
answer.sub: 9 downstream systems across 2 teams — including quote-to-cash and the month-end close, which runs off a nightly Salesforce export with no runbook or owner.

link.label: Hidden Dependency
link.detail: Month-end close depends on a nightly export. No runbook, no owner.

answer.headline: 9 systems · 2 teams
answer.sub: 9 downstream systems across 2 teams — including quote-to-cash and the month-end close, which runs off a nightly Salesforce export with no runbook or owner.

questions.question: Are any patients on dangerous medication combinations?

beats.text: A cardiologist prescribes an anticoagulant.
A primary-care doctor adds a high-dose anti-inflammatory.
Neither saw the other's chart. Together, it's a major bleeding risk.
Filled at different pharmacies, so no single med list ever caught it.

answer.headline: 218 patients
answer.sub: On a major-severity interaction — an anticoagulant and a high-dose NSAID from different providers, filled at different pharmacies.

link.label: Cross-Source Interaction
link.detail: Neither chart saw the other. Major bleeding risk.

answer.headline: 218 patients
answer.sub: On a major-severity interaction — an anticoagulant and a high-dose NSAID from different providers, filled at different pharmacies.

questions.question: Why do our claims keep getting denied?

beats.text: Most denials come back as 'missing prior authorization.'
They look like seven different providers each making mistakes.
But all seven use one order template that turns the auth flag off. One template, not seven people.

answer.headline: $640K
answer.sub: In denials over 90 days (missing prior authorization), all traced to one order-set template that defaults the auth flag off — used by 7 providers.

link.label: Root-Cause Template
link.detail: One template defaults the auth flag off — not seven people.

answer.headline: $640K
answer.sub: In denials over 90 days (missing prior authorization), all traced to one order-set template that defaults the auth flag off — used by 7 providers.
